coredata.dc:title: Construction of CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes and their photocatalytic activity for isoproturon degradation under visible light
coredata.dc:creator: Xie, Ruyi
Zhang, Linping
Liu, Hongchen
Xu, Hong
Zhong, Yi
Sui, Xiaofeng
Mao, Zhiping
coredata.dc:description: The CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts exhibited high visible-light responsive photocatalytic activity. To achieve the cyclic utilization of CQDs-Bi20TiO32, the novel flexible CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN fiber membranes were prepared by a simple coaxial electrospinning technique. It was found that the CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst could be firmly and uniformly fixed on the surfaces of electrospun fibers and the composite fiber membranes possessed the hierarchical macro- and mesoporous structure. The loaded CQDs-Bi20TiO32 endowed the composite fiber with an obvious photocatalytic activity for the degradation of the herbicide isoproturon under visible light irradiation. Among these samples, S3 at photocatalyst dosage 15w/v% had the highest degradation efficiency for isoproturon. Meanwhile, the core-shell structured inorganic-organic hybrid fiber membranes have a significant advantage in the recyclability for photocatalyts. The flexible CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes would have potential applications in efficient removal of organic contaminants.
coredata.dcterms:subject: CQDs-Bi20TiO32
PAN
Coaxial electrospinning
Photocatalysis
Fiber membrane

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: SEM, TEM and HRTEM of the electrospun fiber membranes

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: XRD patterns of (a) PAN electrospun fiber membranes, (b) S1, (c) S2, (d) S3, (e) S4, and (f) CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalyat.
SEM images of electrospun fiber samples: (a) PAN, (b) S1, (c) S2, (d) S3 and (e) S4. (f) TEM of sample S2. (g) HRTEM of CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst. (h) EDX spectrum of sample S2.
FT-IR patterns of CQDs-Bi20TiO32, and electrospun fiber membranes: PAN, S1, S2, S3 and S4.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms and the corresponding BJH pore-size distribution curves (insets) of the electrospun fiber membranes (a) S1, (b) S2, (c) S3 and (d) S4; (e) BET surface area of the electrospun fiber membranes with different photocatalyst contents.
UV–vis DRS spectra of the electrospun fiber membranes.

(A) Degradation rates of isoproturon solutions under visible-light irradiation with different electrospun fiber membranes: PAN, S1, S2, S3 and S4; (B) recycling test of electrospun fiber membranes S3 towards isoproturon degradation.
Mechanism of the isoproturon degradation by CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes.
Experimental parameters for the fabrication of CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN composite fiber membranes.

ce:table-footnote.ce:label: b
ce:table-footnote.ce:note-para: Concentration of CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst in DMAc.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: The contents of CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts on the electrospun fiber membranes.

head.ce:title: Construction of CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes and their photocatalytic activity for isoproturon degradation under visible light

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Lab. of Science and Technology of Eco-Textile, Ministry of Education, College of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Biotechnology, Donghua University, No. 2999 North Renmin Road, Shanghai 201620, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Lab. of Science and Technology of Eco-Textile

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts exhibited high visible-light responsive photocatalytic activity. To achieve the cyclic utilization of CQDs-Bi20TiO32, the novel flexible CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN fiber membranes were prepared by a simple coaxial electrospinning technique. It was found that the CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst could be firmly and uniformly fixed on the surfaces of electrospun fibers and the composite fiber membranes possessed the hierarchical macro- and mesoporous structure. The loaded CQDs-Bi20TiO32 endowed the composite fiber with an obvious photocatalytic activity for the degradation of the herbicide isoproturon under visible light irradiation. Among these samples, S3 at photocatalyst dosage 15w/v% had the highest degradation efficiency for isoproturon. Meanwhile, the core-shell structured inorganic-organic hybrid fiber membranes have a significant advantage in the recyclability for photocatalyts. The flexible CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes would have potential applications in efficient removal of organic contaminants.

ce:section.ce:para: To date, the water contamination caused by various organic pollutants has aroused worldwide public concern. Semiconductor photocatalysis is one of the most promising technologies for wastewater remediation, due to its sustainability, low pollutants selectivity, high-degree mineralization, and environmentally friendly properties [1–3]. To enlarge the using range of solar light, most researches have been focused on the development of visible-light-responsive photocatalysts, such as Bi-based [4], Ag-based [5], W-based [6], In-based [7,8], and Fe-based [9] photocatalysts. Bi20TiO32, a novel visible-light-responsive photocatalyst, has attracted extensive scientific interest in photocatalytic degradation of organic pollutants. Owing to the hybridized valence band by Bi 6s and O 2p orbitals, Bi20TiO32 has a relatively narrow band-gap energy 2.36eV, corresponding to the light absorption edge at 525nm [10]. Nevertheless, its photocatalytic activity is still inhibited by the high recombination rate of photo-generated electron-hole pairs, resulting in the low utilization efficiency of photons. To solve this issue, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were introduced into Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts via solvothermal synthesis and oil bath reflux method in our previous report [11]. Due to the excellent electron transfer ability and up-converting photoluminescence of CQDs, the separation rate of electron-hole pairs in CQDs-Bi20TiO32 composite photocatalyst was obviously enhanced. So CQDs-Bi20TiO32 composite exhibited higher photocatalytic activity for the degradation of the herbicide isoproturon than pure Bi20TiO32. But it is also a challenge for the photocatalysts in powder form to be reused in practical application.

Electrospinning has received an increasing appeal as a versatile technique to produce fibers with diameters in the range from micrometers to tens of nanometers. The electrospun fiber membranes possess several fascinating features including large surface area, high porosity and light weight [12,13]. By virtue of the merits of such a technique, a variety of one-dimensional (1D) photocatalysts nanofibers have been successfully fabricated, such as ZnO [14], Nb2O5 [15], BiVO4 [16,17], In2S3/TiO2 [18], and CuO/TiO2 [19]. The 1D mesoporous architectures supply more surface active sites for the photocatalytic reaction [20,21], but the inevitable brittleness is another problem for the inorganic photocatalyst fibers in the recycling process. Introduced approximately in 2003 [22,23], coaxial electrospinning technology could combine two different components together to form core-shell structured micro-nanometer scale fibers. If loading the photocatalysts uniformly on the surface of organic polymer fiber by this approach, the resulting flexible composite fiber membranes may get potential applications in environmental purification.

Herein, the flexible CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes were successfully prepared through the coaxial electrospinning technology. The CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts were firmly fixed on the fiber surface and retained good photocatalytic activity. The phase structures, morphologies, optical properties and pore structures of the fiber membranes were well characterized. The visible-light photocatalytic activity and recyclability of the samples were evaluated by the degradation of isoproturon, a phenylurea herbicide with low toxicity. Furthermore, the possible photo-degradation mechanism of isoproturon by the composite fiber membranes was put forward.

Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O), ethylenediamine (H2NCH2CH2NH2), bismuth nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), benzyl alcohol (C7H8O), and absolute ethanol (CH3CH2OH) were purchased from the Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. Tetraisopropyl orthotitanate (Ti(OC3H7)4) was procured from the Tokyo Chemical Industry Co., Ltd. N,N-Dimethylacetamide (DMAc) was obtained from the Shanghai Lingfeng Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. All of the above chemicals were of analytical grade and used as received without further purification. Isoproturon (>99.6% pure, HPLC grade) was purchased from the AccuStandard, USA. Polyacrylonitrile (PAN, MW
=60,000) was supplied by the Zhejiang Shangyu Wuyue Economic and Trade Co., Ltd.

The photocatalyst CQDs-Bi20TiO32 was prepared according to our previous report [11]. The CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN fiber membranes were fabricated by coaxial electrospinning technology. The sheath fluid was prepared with PAN, CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst and DMAc solvent. The concentration of PAN in DMAc was set to 10w/v% and the contents of CQDs-Bi20TiO32 aspect to DMAc were adjusted to 5w/v%, 10w/v%, 15w/v% and 20w/v%, respectively. The detailed process was described as follows. The fixed amount of PAN powder and appropriate amounts of photocatalysts were added into DMAc. The mixture was vigorously stirred at 75°C for 6h to form sheath fluids. The core solution with concentration PAN 15w/v% was prepared in the same way without using the photocatalyst. Subsequently, the core and sheath solutions were loaded into two syringes connected to a concentric nozzle (inner and outer diameters were 0.8 and 1.2mm, respectively). A stable voltage of 20kV was applied to the concentric nozzle by the high voltage supply (ZGF-60KV/5MA, Shanghai Sute Electrical Corp., Ltd. China). The core and sheath fluids were pumped out at rates of 0.3mL/h and 0.4mL/h using two syringe pumps (KDS100, Cole-Parmer, USA). The resultant electrospun fiber membranes were collected on the aluminum foil covering on the grounded metallic collector. The distance from the nozzle to collector was set to 20cm. The temperature and relative humidity during electrospinning processes were maintained at 25±5°C and 35±5%, respectively. The as-prepared fiber membranes were dried in an oven at 100°C overnight to remove the residual solvent. To achieve the optimum photocatalyst coating on the PAN fiber, the same electrospinning procedure was carried out using the same core fluid and four different sheath fluids (the contents of CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts aspect to the solvent were 5w/v%, 10w/v%, 15w/v% and 20w/v%, respectively). Correspondingly, the obtained fiber membranes were labeled S1, S2, S3 and S4. The detailed preparation parameters and corresponding properties of composite fiber membranes were listed in Table 1 . For comparison, bare PAN nanofiber mat was also prepared by the similar method without using photocatalysts.

The phase structures of the prepared samples were characterized using X-ray diffraction (XRD, Rigaku Ltd., Japan) with Cu Ka (k=1.54056Å). The Bi content on these composite fibers was determined by inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometry (ICP-AES, Leeman Prodigy, USA) according to general rules (JY/T 015-1996). Then the loading content of CQDs-Bi20TiO32 on PAN fibers was calculated based on the mass fraction of Bi element. The morphologies and elementary compositions of CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN fiber membranes were observed by scanning electron microscopy (SEM, TM-1000, HITACHI, Japan) and energy dispersive X-ray spectrometer (EDS, X-Max Oxford Instruments). The microstructures were investigated by a transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEM-2100, JEOL, Japan) and a high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM, JEM-2100F, JEOL, Japan). The UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy (UV-vis DRS) were examined on a UV–vis spectrophotometer (Lambda 35, PerkinElmer) in the range of 200–800nm. Fourier Transform Infrared spectra (FT-IR) were obtained using a Nicolet Nexus 670 FTIR spectrometer with KBr pellets. The Brunauer-Emmett-Teller (BET) surface area and porous structures of the samples were analyzed by nitrogen adsorption-desorption measurements at 77K using a Micromeritics Tristar II 3020 system.

The photocatalytic activities of CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes were investigated based on the photo-degradation of the herbicide isoproturon under visible light irradiation. A 500W Xe lamp with a 400nm cutoff filter provided the visible light source. The photocatalysis reactions were carried out in a quartz reaction cell at a temperature of 25±5°C. The round CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes (8cm in diameter and approximately 0.1g in weight) were put into petri dishes (diameter: 9cm, height: 1.5cm) with 50mL of 15mg/L isoproturon solution. The distance between light source and the center of petri dishes was approximately 5cm. During the photocatalytic reaction, fixed amounts of the reaction solution were withdrawn at given time intervals and centrifuged to remove the impurities. The concentration of the isoproturon solution was analyzed by the Agilent-1100 high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) with a C-18 Phenomenex reverse phase column. The test wavelength was set to 239nm. The mobile phase was a mixture of acetonitrile and water with a volume ratio of 50:50 at a flow rate of 1mL/min, and 20μL of the sample was injected each time. The used membranes were collected and dried overnight at 100°C in an oven. To investigate the recyclability of the CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN fiber membranes, the regenerated fiber membranes were reused under the same conditions for 4 cycles. In each cycle, sample S3 was irradiated under visible light for 60h and was then removed from the isoproturon solution. After being washed with deionized water, S3 was reused in the next cycle. The degradation process of isoproturon was also monitored by HPLC under the same condition.

The phase compositions of CQDs-Bi20TiO32, pristine PAN and CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes were characterized by XRD. As depicted in Fig. 1 (a), only one broad diffraction peak between 20° and 30° appeared in the XRD pattern of the bare PAN fiber mat, indicating its amorphous form [24–26]. This phenomenon can be attributed to the fact that the electrospinning processes took place in very short time and the PAN molecules did not have enough time to crystallize. From Fig. 1(f), the diffraction peaks of CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts were well matched to Bi20TiO32 phases and no obvious peaks of CQDs were found due to their amorphous nature and low content [27,28]. The main diffractions peaks at 2θ=28.2°, 31.62°, 32.88°, 46.26°, 47.18°, 54.16°, 55.77°, 57.91°,74.61° and 75.94° corresponded to the (201), (002), (220), (222), (400), (203), (421), (402), (423) and (601) crystal planes of the tetragonal Bi20TiO32 phase (JCPDS42-0202), respectively. With respect to the CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes in Fig. 1(b–e), the intensity of main diffraction peaks indexed to CQDs-Bi20TiO32 increased gradually with the increase amounts of photocatalysts. Additionally, these peaks become much broader due to the interaction between photocatalysts and amorphous PAN macromolecules. The XRD patterns could confirm the successful introduction of the CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst on PAN electrospun fibers.

The contents of Bi element on the composite fiber membranes were analyzed by ICP-AES and the results were listed in Table 2 . No Bi element was detected in the PAN fiber membranes. For CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes, the contents of Bi increased gradually from S1 to S4. At the same time, the contents of CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts could be calculated to be 12.5%, 21.2%, 28.4% and 38.4% for S1, S2, S3 and S4 samples, respectively.

ce:section.ce:section-title: SEM, TEM and HRTEM of the electrospun fiber membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The surface morphologies and microstructures of the electrospun fiber membranes were examined by SEM, TEM and HRTEM. The SEM images in Fig. 2 showed both bare PAN and CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes presented randomly arranged porous structure. From Fig. 2(a), the bare PAN electrospun fibers had relatively smooth surfaces, were distributed uniformly with an average diameter of approximately 543nm. In contrast, many particles anchored on the surface of CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fibers as shown in Fig. 2(b–e). With the increment of photocatalyst usage, the CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN samples exhibited much rougher surfaces. Meanwhile, the average diameter of the electrospun fibers increased gradually from 665nm (S1) to 1069nm (S4). This could be explained by the reason that the addition of CQDs-Bi20TiO32 led to a higher viscosity of sheath fluid, which inhibited the stretch of the fibers during the electrospinning process [29]. In this system, electrospun fibers could not form when the amount of photocatalysts in the sheath fluid exceeded 20w/v%. The distribution of the photocatalyst on the electrospun fibers in sample S2 was further studied by TEM and HRTEM images in Fig. 3 (f) and (g). It can be seen clearly from Fig. 3(f) that the PAN fiber kept amorphous state and the CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts were distributed evenly in the PAN matrix. A HRTEM image of CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst was taken for the observation of its microstructure. As shown in Fig. 2(h), two distinctively different fringes can be observed for the photocatalyst. The lattice spacing of 0.31nm corresponds to the (201) crystal plane of Bi20TiO32 [30]. The other lattice spacing of 0.21nm matches with the (100) crystal plane of CQDs [31]. The HRTEM images could demonstrate the presence of carbon quantum dots on the photocatalyst. The semi-quantitative EDS spectrum analysis of S2 in Fig. 2(h) implied the coexistence of C, N, O, Bi, and Ti elements on the fiber surface. The results further verified the surface decoration of composite electrospun fibers by CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts, which was in consistent with the XRD results.

FTIR spectra of CQDs-Bi20TiO32 composite, bare PAN and composite electrospun fibers were depicted in Fig. 3. For CQDs-Bi20TiO32 composite, the absorption peaks could be observed as follows: vibrational absorption band of CO at 1635cm−1, bending vibrations of NH at 1570cm−1, stretching vibration of TiO at 820cm−1 [32], stretching vibration of BiO at 635cm−1 and 514cm−1 [33]. Regarding the FTIR spectrum of bare PAN electrospun fibers, the absorption peaks at 2927cm−1, 2244cm−1, 1450cm−1, C1386cm−1 and 1240cm−1 corresponded to stretching vibration of methylene CH2 , stretching vibration of CN, bending vibration of CH2 , bending vibration of CH, and stretching vibration of CC, respectively. For CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN composite electrospun fibers, the characteristic absorption peaks of CQDs-Bi20TiO32 at 820cm−1, 635cm−1 and 514cm−1 appeared and strengthened gradually with the increasing amount of the photocatalyst. The FT-IR results further demonstrated the loading of CQDs-Bi20TiO32 on PAN fibers.

Nitrogen adsorption-desorption measurements were conducted to investigate the porous distribution and specific surface areas of CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes, and the results are shown in Fig. 4 . The types of nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms were analyzed according to the IUPAC classification [34]. The nitrogen adsorption-desorption isotherms of all samples in Fig. 4(a–d) exhibited the typical type-IV curve with a H3-type hysteresis loop, revealing the presence of mesoporous structure [35,36]. In addition, the nitrogen adsorption increased sharply at high relative pressures (P/P0 >0.9) confirming the existence of macropores [37]. The plot of the pore size distribution (inset in Fig. 4(a–d)) was acquired by the Barrett-Joyner-Halenda (BJH) method. The majority of pores for all samples were in the range of mesopores and macropores. The CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes possessed the hierarchical macro- and mesoporous structure. In Fig. 4(e), the BET specific surface area of the fiber membranes reached the maximum value 11.18m2/g for S3 and decreased for S4 with more photocatalysts. It was because that the BET specific surface area was affected by both fiber surface roughness and fiber diameter. The addition of photocatalysts could roughen the fiber surfaces, but at the same time made the fiber much thicker as shown in Fig. 2. The hierarchical porous architecture and enhanced BET surface area of the composite fiber membranes were beneficial for removal of organic pollutants.

Fig. 5 shows the UV–vis diffuse reflectance spectroscopy of bare PAN and CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes. The bare PAN electrospun fiber mats showed no obvious absorption at wavelengths greater than 300nm. After CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts incorporated to the PAN matrix, the absorption intensities of the fiber membranes in the UV–vis light region were dramatically enhanced. The absorption edge of the composite fiber membranes was extremely extended to a longer wavelength in visible light range with the increase of photocatalyst contents. The CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst endowed the composite fiber membranes with remarkable visible-light responsive property. It meant that under the irradiation of sunlight CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes would exhibit higher photocatalytic activity than bare PAN fiber membrane.

The degradation of herbicide isoproturon under visible light irradiation was conducted to evaluate the photocatalytic activity of electrospun composite fiber membranes, and the results are presented in Fig. 6 . In the photolysis experiment without CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN fiber membranes, no prominent change in the isoproturon concentration was noticed after irradiation for 60h, indicating the good photostability of isoproturon. After adding the bare PAN electrospun fiber membrane, only approximately 30.7% of isoproturon could be adsorbed. As for the CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes, the removal efficiencies of isoproturon for different samples S1, S2, S3 and S4 were 48.6%, 72.7%, 90.4% and 80.3%, respectively. The introduction of CQDs-Bi20TiO32 on the PAN fiber surface led to more prominent photocatalytic activities for degradation of isoproturon. Nevertheless, excessive amounts of photocatalysts could cause the increase of fiber diameter, which reduced the contact area between isoproturon molecules and CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts. In this system, the sample S3 at photocatalyst dosage 15w/v% showed the highest degradation efficiency.

The recyclability of the electrospun fiber mats was further investigated using sample S3. As presented in Fig. 6(B), sample S3 did not exhibit a significant loss of photocatalytic activity throughout four cycles indicating the good stability of the composite fiber membrane. The slight decline in the photo-degradation rate for isoproturon could be attributed to the inevitable photo-corrosion of the photocatalyst under the irradiation for such a long time [38,39]. The remarkable recyclability of CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes has potential application in toxic waste water treatment.

The degradation process of isoproturon by CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes was illustrated in Fig. 7 . According to the above experimental results and our previous study on CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalyst, the removal of isoproturon could mainly involve the following three typical stages: (i) Electrospun fiber membranes with hierarchical macro- and mesoporous structure facilitated the isoproturon molecules to be concentrated on the fiber surface. (ii) The CQDs-Bi20TiO32 photocatalysts on the fiber surface absorbed visible-light energy to generate eCB − and hVB + in the CB and VB of Bi20TiO32. The eCB − would transfer to the CQDs and further react with O2 to form O2 −. (iii) The isoproturon molecules were completely degraded by the active species hVB + and O2 −. Then, the composite fiber membranes would be used in the next cycle.

CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN photocatalytic fiber membranes were successfully prepared via the coaxial electrospinning technology. The SEM, TEM, and HRTEM images showed that CQDs-Bi20TiO32 nanoparticles were uniformly anchored on the composite fiber surfaces. The composite fiber membranes possessed the hierarchical macro- and mesoporous structure. The loaded photocatalysts endowed the fiber membranes with obvious visible light responsive property. They displayed excellent photocatalytic activity for the degradation of isoproturon under visible light irradiation. The sample S3 at photocatalyst dosage 15w/v% exhibited the highest isopruturon degradation performance with a removal efficiency of 90.4%. Moreover, the composite fiber membranes have good stability, so the efficient photocatalysts could be reused easily in degradation of the herbicide isoproturon. The functional CQDs-Bi20TiO32/PAN electrospun fiber membranes may have promising applications in water/air purification.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by the National Key Technology R & D Program (No. 2014BAC13B02) and the Scientific Research Foundation for the Returned Overseas Chinese Scholars, State Education Ministry (No. 13W10539).
